findings.message: `RunReviewFixInteractor::run` returns the injected closure's `RunReviewFixOutput` unchanged, so the service can successfully surface an arbitrary status or a mismatched exit code such as `status = "completed"` with `exit_code = 2`. The usecase contract limits results to `completed`/`blocked_cross_scope`/`failed` with fixed exit-code mapping, so the boundary should validate the returned DTO and convert invalid output to an error before returning.

`review_run_fix_local()` collapses every `ReviewFixRunnerError` into `RunReviewFixError::FixRunnerFailed`, so `CodexReviewFixRunner::SmokeTestFailed` never reaches the dedicated `RunReviewFixError::SmokeTestFailed` variant. That breaks the service error taxonomy and hides smoke-test/environment/version failures behind a generic runner failure.

`review_run_fix_local` forwards `scope` and `track_id` into `RunReviewFixCommand` without any of the existing domain validation used by `review_run_local`. The interactor only checks for non-empty strings, so malformed values can reach `CodexReviewFixRunner::build_prompt` and be interpolated directly into the nested agent prompt and orchestration text.

`make_track_local_review_fix_codex` uses `strip_leading_separator`, which tokenizes a single `${@}` payload by whitespace instead of shell words. Quoted arguments and paths with spaces (for example `--briefing-file '/tmp/a b.md'` or `--scope-files 'x y.rs'`) will be split incorrectly before they reach `sotp review fix-local`.

The shell-word parser used here drops literal backslashes inside double quotes. For a single raw arg like `--briefing-file "tmp/a\b.md"`, this rewrites the value to `tmp/ab.md` instead of preserving the backslash, so quoted paths or regexes with backslashes are corrupted before being forwarded to `sotp review fix-local`.

Mapping every non-smoke-test `ReviewFixRunnerError` through `other.to_string()` leaks infrastructure details to CLI users. `SentinelNotFound` and `Unexpected` can include the temporary session log path and other internal execution details, and `CliApp::review_run_fix_local` re-emits them verbatim as `[ERROR] ...`, which violates the repo's no-internal-details rule for error messages.

The new public `review_run_fix_local` / `make_track_local_review_fix_codex` entry points have no direct happy-path or error-path tests in this scope. The only added tests exercise the private shell-word helper, so provider resolution, unsupported-provider rejection, and `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS` propagation can regress without coverage.

`dispatch_track_local_review_fix_codex()` is not a transparent passthrough for valid cargo-make inputs: it re-tokenizes `${@}` with `split_whitespace`, so quoted `--briefing-file` or `--scope-files` values containing spaces are split apart before Clap sees them. The new make task will misparse those legitimate paths and can invoke `sotp review fix-local` with the wrong arguments.

The new `sotp review fix-local` public entry point ships without any happy-path or error-path tests covering `FixLocalArgs` -> `RunReviewFixLocalInput` mapping and `RunReviewFixOutput.exit_code` propagation. That leaves regressions in the new CLI wiring and exit-code behavior unguarded, which violates the repo's testing requirement for public APIs.

The shell-word parser drops every backslash inside double quotes, so a legitimate quoted value like `--briefing-file "tmp/a\b.md"` is forwarded as `tmp/ab.md`. That silently corrupts path-like arguments in the new make passthrough.

The prompt is written to a tempfile here, but that file is never passed to Codex. `build_codex_fixer_invocation()` still sends the entire prompt as the final argv item, and `stdin` is closed, so the supposed mitigation for long/multiline prompts is not actually used. Large briefing/scope prompts can still hit argv-size or quoting failures.

The temporary HOME directory is derived only from the process ID (`review-fix-codex-home-<pid>`). Concurrent `run_fix()` calls in the same process will reuse the same directory, so one run can overwrite or delete another run's isolated HOME contents. The bash wrapper used a unique `mktemp -d` directory, so this changes the isolation semantics.

ASDF-managed Codex installs can break after `env_clear()` because `ASDF_DATA_DIR` and `ASDF_DIR` are only forwarded when they already exist in the parent environment. The old bash wrapper always defaulted both to `~/.asdf`, so on a shell that exposes `codex` via an asdf shim but does not export those variables, `resolve_codex_bin_path()` can hand this runner the shim path and the child can fail to launch with exit 126.

`parse_sentinel()` trims each line before matching, so malformed lines like `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS: completed ` or `  REVIEW_FIX_STATUS: completed` are accepted even though the contract and the previous bash parser require an exact full-line match. That weakens the sentinel gate and can classify invalid output as a valid result.

`build_prompt()` rejects an empty `scope_files` list with `Unexpected("scope_files must not be empty")`, but the public fixer DTO and CLI accept `--scope-files` as zero-or-more and the usecase layer does not validate this earlier. A valid `review fix-local` invocation with no scope files therefore fails late inside the infrastructure adapter instead of being handled as a structured validation outcome.

`build_prompt()` rejects an empty `scope_files` list even though the public `fix-local` CLI accepts `--scope-files` as zero-or-more (`num_args = 0..`) and the usecase layer does not validate this earlier. A valid invocation with no scope files therefore fails late inside the infrastructure adapter as `Unexpected` instead of being handled as a structured validation outcome.

The production portion of this module is 894 lines before `#[cfg(test)]`, which exceeds the repository's 700-line module cap. This adapter needs to be split into smaller production modules before it can land.

`run_fix()` builds the prompt from `self.scope`, `self.briefing_file`, and `self.scope_files` instead of the `RunReviewFixCommand` it receives. That makes the adapter ignore per-invocation scope/boundary data and can produce a prompt for stale configuration if the caller changes those fields.

`build_codex_fixer_invocation()` never appends the generated prompt to `argv`, and `spawn_and_collect_codex()` only feeds the prompt through stdin. The other Codex wrappers in this repo pass the task/prompt positionally, so this adapter is likely launching `codex exec` without the actual task text and can hang or run an empty session.

`build_prompt()` inserts `briefing_file.display()` and every `scope_files` path directly into the Codex instructions without validating or escaping them. A path containing newlines or backticks can inject extra instructions and rewrite the modification boundary, so the file-scope guard is not actually enforced by the adapter.

If `stdin.write_all(prompt)` fails, `spawn_and_collect_codex()` kills the child and returns `SpawnFailed` before writing `tmp/reviewer-runtime/review-fix-codex-session-*.log`. That drops the stdout/stderr transcript for a failure case the briefing explicitly requires to be preserved.

PATH prefixing only happens when the parent process already has a `PATH` value. If this runner is invoked from an environment without `PATH`, the child gets no `PATH` at all, so the prepended codex bin directory cannot help it discover the sibling `node` runtime and `codex exec` can fail to start.

`build_prompt()` injects `scope` into the embedded `cargo make ... --group ...` command verbatim, but `ReviewGroupName::try_new()` only trims whitespace and still accepts spaces. A valid scope like `"usecase cli"` will split the reviewer invocation and the nested fixer will be launched with the wrong arguments.

`build_codex_fixer_invocation()` interpolates `codex_home_str` directly into the `sandbox_workspace_write.writable_roots=["..."]` config string without escaping. Valid `HOME`/`CODEX_HOME` values containing `"` or `\` will produce malformed Codex config and prevent the nested fixer from starting.

`resolve_codex_bin_path()` and the version smoke-test run before `build_safe_env()` reconstructs `ASDF_DIR`/`ASDF_DATA_DIR`, so an environment that relies on the default `~/.asdf` layout but does not export those vars can fail to resolve the real `codex` binary. In that case the code can fall back to the `asdf` shim or fail the smoke-test even though the later fixer subprocess would have had the needed ASDF env, which makes launch behavior depend on ambient shell state instead of the runner's own isolation policy.

`smoke_test_codex_version()` runs `codex --version` without the same PATH prefix that `run_fix()` later applies to the real subprocess. For ASDF/npm-style installs that need a sibling `node` on PATH, this can fail the smoke test and reject a valid Codex installation before the actual execution path is tried.

`path_with_optional_prefix()` hard-codes `:` when prepending the Codex binary directory to PATH. That works on Unix, but it produces an invalid PATH on Windows, so the fixer will not be able to find the resolved binary's sibling runtime there.

Even excluding the test-only sections, the production part of this module is still above the repository's 700-line cap. This adapter needs to be split into smaller production modules before it can land.

`run_fix()` has a direct happy-path test, but no adapter-level negative test for the failure contract (`SentinelNotFound` / spawn failure). That leaves the newly exported public API without the required error-case coverage.

The prompt body interpolates `track_id`, `scope`, and `reviewer_model` verbatim into the nested agent instructions (`## Orchestrator Assignment`). Those values are only minimally validated upstream, so a newline/backtick payload can splice extra markdown or instructions into the fixer prompt and weaken the file-boundary guard.
Even with the `#[cfg(test)]` block excluded, the production portion of this module runs to 702 lines, which exceeds the repository's 700-line cap for a single module.

`parse_sentinel` treats whitespace-only lines as non-empty, so an otherwise valid final message like `REVIEW_FIX_STATUS: completed\n   \n` is rejected as `SentinelNotFound`. The bash implementation explicitly ignored whitespace-only trailing lines, and this false negative can make a completed fixer run fail the orchestrator gate.

The ADR states that `network_access=true` opens only the nested reviewer’s LLM reachability, but D2 enables network on the outer fixer sandbox, so every process the fixer runs can make outbound network calls. Since the same design also makes `CODEX_HOME`/`~/.codex` writable for session creation, this overstates the safety boundary: push prevention may still hold, but general outbound access is broader than LLM-only access and can mislead the implementation plan’s security assumptions.

`plan.md`/`impl-plan.json` present T001 as only in progress, but the generated `usecase-types.md` now marks every T001 usecase catalogue entry as Blue instead of Yellow. In this workflow Yellow is the WIP declaration state and Blue is the implemented/confirmed state, so the plan artifacts now give conflicting status signals for the same T001 work and can falsely present the usecase contract as already complete.
